The Iran Conflict & Your Financial Position

A personalized economic outlook for navigating the largest oil supply disruption since the 1970s

Prepared ForClient — Crescent City, CA DateMarch 19, 2026 ClassificationConfidential AdvisorSenior Wealth Strategy
Confidential — For Intended Recipient Only

Executive Summary

The U.S.–Israeli military campaign against Iran, launched February 28, 2026, has triggered the largest oil supply disruption in modern history. The effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz has removed roughly 20% of global oil supply from the market, sending Brent crude from $70 to over $100 per barrel in under three weeks. For you — a tech-sector professional in rural Northern California with concentrated exposure in technology equities and local real estate — this conflict creates a distinct risk profile that requires careful attention. This report examines the macroeconomic landscape, assesses the specific implications for your three primary financial pillars, and offers actionable recommendations.

Section 01

The Macro Picture: What Happened & Why It Matters

Understanding the scale of the disruption is essential to evaluating its downstream effects on your portfolio and daily life.

On February 28, 2026, the United States and Israel launched coordinated airstrikes against Iranian military infrastructure and leadership targets, killing Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei and initiating what the administration has called "Operation Epic Fury." Iran retaliated with missile and drone strikes against Israel, U.S. military bases across the Gulf, and — critically — commercial shipping in the Strait of Hormuz.

The Strait of Hormuz is the single most important chokepoint in the global energy system. Roughly 20 million barrels of oil and petroleum products transit it daily — approximately one-fifth of global supply. The International Energy Agency has called this the largest supply disruption in the history of the global oil market.

Fig. 1 Brent crude oil price trajectory and projected scenarios based on historical analogues

The conflict has also spread beyond Iran. Hezbollah has re-entered the conflict in Lebanon; Iranian-allied groups have struck targets in Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, Jordan, and Qatar; QatarEnergy has suspended LNG production; and airspace closures across the Middle East have grounded thousands of flights and disrupted global aviation routes.

As of this writing, the war is in its 19th day with no ceasefire in sight. Israel has continued targeted assassinations of senior Iranian officials, and Iran has vowed continued retaliation. The Strait of Hormuz remains effectively closed to commercial shipping.

Section 02

Energy Prices & Cost of Living in Crescent City

Rural Northern California is disproportionately exposed to fuel price shocks due to distance from supply infrastructure and car-dependent daily life.

The national average gasoline price has risen to approximately $3.72 per gallon, up nearly 80 cents from a month ago. Diesel has approached $5 per gallon nationally, a level not seen since Russia's invasion of Ukraine in 2022. In California — which carries higher fuel taxes and stricter blend requirements — prices surged above $5 per gallon during the second week of March and are trending higher with the seasonal switch to summer-blend gasoline.

Your location in Crescent City amplifies this pressure significantly. Del Norte County sits at the end of long supply chains from the nearest refineries. Historically, fuel prices in this region run 20–40 cents above the California average, and that gap tends to widen during supply disruptions. Beyond fuel, virtually everything consumed locally — groceries, building materials, household goods — arrives by truck. Higher diesel costs flow directly into the price of those goods.

Fig. 2 Gasoline price comparison: national, state, and local impact disparity
Key Watchpoint

Fuel costs account for 40–50% of all variable costs of growing crops in the U.S. The Gulf region also supplies a significant share of global fertilizer production, and one-third of world fertilizer supply transits the Strait of Hormuz. If disruptions persist through spring planting, food prices could rise substantially later this year — hitting rural, food-importing communities like Crescent City especially hard.

Section 03

Your Technology Stock Exposure

The tech sector is experiencing a divergence — defense-adjacent names are surging while the broader Magnificent Seven have entered correction territory.

The Bloomberg index tracking the "Magnificent Seven" mega-cap tech stocks closed more than 10% below its October record on March 13, officially entering correction territory. The Nasdaq overall has fallen roughly 2.4% since the war began. However, the picture within technology is far from uniform, and what you hold matters enormously.

Fig. 3 Tech sector performance divergence since onset of Iran conflict
Segment Direction Key Drivers Risk Level
Defense Tech (Palantir, CrowdStrike) ↑ Up 6–12% DoD contracts, cyber warfare demand, data analytics Low
Semiconductors (Nvidia, TSMC exposure) ↓ Under pressure Helium/bromine supply risk from Gulf; Asian hub disruptions Moderate–High
Mega-Cap / Mag Seven ↓ In correction AI capex uncertainty compounded by energy shock Moderate
Software / SaaS ↓ Weakest subsector AI disruption to licensing models + macro headwinds Elevated
Cloud / Middle East Buildout ↓ Disrupted Iranian retaliatory attacks hit Gulf data centers; project delays High
Concentration Risk Alert

With the majority of your investments in tech stocks, your portfolio carries meaningful concentration risk during this period. If you are heavily weighted toward the Magnificent Seven or software names, your drawdown may already exceed 10%. Morgan Stanley has recommended selectively rotating into healthcare, industrials, and materials for clients with similar profiles.

Section 04

Local Real Estate Outlook

Del Norte County real estate operates on different fundamentals than major metros, offering some insulation — but not immunity.

Crescent City's housing market is driven primarily by local employment (fishing, timber, government, tourism), affordability-seeking retirees, and a limited housing supply. These dynamics provide a degree of insulation from the forces battering national housing markets.

However, the interest rate environment is working against property values broadly. Mortgage rates have risen from 5.99% immediately before the war to 6.29% by mid-March, and further increases are possible if inflation remains elevated and the Federal Reserve holds firm — or even hikes. Higher rates directly reduce the purchasing power of would-be buyers.

The more nuanced risk for your Crescent City holdings is second-order: if the broader economy tips into stagflation, discretionary relocation to rural communities slows, tourism spending declines, and public-sector budgets may tighten. Del Norte County's economy is sensitive to all three of these channels.

Silver Lining

Property values in the Crescent City area remain well below California medians, meaning absolute dollar exposure is limited relative to, say, Bay Area holdings. Additionally, if the conflict accelerates the shift to renewable energy and EV adoption — as several analysts project — Northern California's abundant hydroelectric and wind resources could make the region modestly more attractive over the medium term.

Section 05

Your Tech Career: Employment Risk Assessment

The war adds a layer of uncertainty to a tech labor market already navigating AI-driven restructuring.

The technology sector entered 2026 in a cautious hiring posture. While AI investment has created new roles, it has also pressured traditional software positions. The Iran conflict compounds this by introducing macroeconomic uncertainty that tends to make businesses delay hiring and investment decisions.

If you work remotely for a company based elsewhere — which is common for tech workers in Crescent City — the key question is your employer's sensitivity to the current environment. Larger, profitable companies with diversified revenue are likely to maintain headcount. Venture-funded startups or mid-stage companies dependent on capital raises face a more precarious environment, especially if rising interest rates and market volatility make investors more cautious.

Companies with significant exposure to Gulf-region AI infrastructure projects (data center buildouts in UAE, Saudi Arabia, etc.) may see revenue deferrals as those projects are paused amid regional instability.

Section 06

Scenario Analysis: Where This Goes From Here

The duration of the conflict — and specifically how quickly the Strait of Hormuz reopens — is the dominant variable shaping outcomes.

Fig. 4 U.S. inflation trajectory under base-case and stress scenarios
Scenario Oil Price U.S. Inflation U.S. GDP Growth Probability
Quick Resolution (weeks) ~$85–90/bbl ~3% peak ~2.25% Moderate
Prolonged (months) $100–120/bbl 4–5% peak ~1.0% Moderate
Extended / Escalation $140+/bbl ~5%+ peak Near zero Lower

Capital Economics forecasts that if the war ends within a few weeks, the U.S. economy would still grow approximately 2.25% this year, with inflation peaking around 3%. Under the worst-case scenario modeled by Oxford Economics — oil sustained at $140/barrel — inflation could reach 5% in Q2 2026, the highest since early 2023, potentially forcing the Fed to hike rates and bringing economic growth to a near standstill.

Fig. 5 The Strait of Hormuz: anatomy of the world's most critical energy chokepoint

Section 08

Recommended Actions

Practical steps to reduce risk and position for multiple outcomes. These are strategic considerations, not guarantees — all investment involves risk.

01

Audit Your Tech Concentration

Review your current holdings line by line. Identify your exposure to the Magnificent Seven, software, and any names with Middle East infrastructure revenue. Consider whether your risk tolerance still matches a portfolio that may be 80%+ in one sector during a period of elevated volatility.

02

Consider Selective Diversification

You don't need to exit tech entirely, but this is a reasonable time to trim overweight positions and allocate a portion toward energy, healthcare, industrials, or defensive dividend-paying equities. Gold and REITs have also shown resilience during energy-driven shocks. Morgan Stanley and Schwab are both recommending similar rotations for concentrated tech portfolios.

03

Build a Cash Buffer

With gas prices rising sharply and the cost of goods climbing in a remote area like Crescent City, now is a prudent time to ensure you have 4–6 months of living expenses in accessible cash or near-cash equivalents. This also protects against potential employment disruption.

04

Hold Your Real Estate

Unless you need liquidity, this is not the moment to sell Crescent City real estate. Transaction costs are high, buyer pools are thin during rate spikes, and local fundamentals haven't deteriorated. Patience is the right posture here.

05

Reduce Fuel Exposure Where Possible

This is tactical, not strategic — but consolidating errands, exploring fuel cost-sharing, or even evaluating whether an EV makes sense at current gas-vs-electricity economics in your area could meaningfully reduce your monthly burn rate in a $5+/gallon environment.

06

Assess Your Employer's Exposure

Have a clear-eyed view of your company's financial health, client base, and exposure to the current disruption. If the company is venture-funded, understand the runway. If it has Gulf-region revenue, understand the contingency plan. This isn't about panic — it's about not being caught off guard.

Closing

Final Perspective

History tells us that oil shocks and military conflicts, while painful, tend to be absorbed by the U.S. economy over a period of months — not years. The S&P 500 has historically recovered within six months of major geopolitical events. The U.S. is also the world's largest oil producer, which provides a structural buffer that didn't exist during the 1970s crises.

That said, your particular combination of assets — tech-concentrated investments, a tech-sector career, and real estate in a remote, supply-chain-dependent community — creates a correlation risk that warrants active management during this period. The recommendations above are designed not to predict a single outcome but to ensure you are resilient across a range of scenarios.

We will continue to monitor the situation closely and will schedule a follow-up review as conditions evolve. Please don't hesitate to reach out with questions.
